---
title: "Chapter Title: Concrete Problem Statement"
---

<!-- SOTA CHAPTER TEMPLATE
     This template demonstrates state-of-the-art content quality patterns:
     1. Incident-driven opening with specific numbers
     2. Production-ready code examples
     3. Professional diagrams (D2 or Mermaid)
     4. Interactive calculators (Observable JS)
     5. Quantified outcomes

     USAGE:
     1. Copy this template to your book's chapters/ directory
     2. Replace placeholders with your content
     3. Customize the incident, code, diagram, and calculator for your scenario
     4. Test: make render ebook=<slug>

     SEE ALSO:
     - docs/D2_DIAGRAM_GUIDE.md — How to create diagrams
     - docs/OBSERVABLE_JS_PATTERNS.md — How to add calculators
     - docs/CONTENT_QUALITY.md — SOTA content standards
-->

<!-- IMPORTANT: Do NOT add a body-level H1 heading (e.g., # Chapter Title).
     The YAML 'title:' field above already generates the chapter heading in Quarto books.
     Adding a duplicate H1 causes broken section numbering. -->

<!-- SECTION 1: INCIDENT-DRIVEN OPENING
     Start with a specific incident that sets up the "why" for this chapter.
     Requirements:
     - Real numbers ($X/month, N instances, X% utilization)
     - Specific organization (anonymized is fine)
     - Quantified problem or pain point
     - 1-2 paragraphs max
-->

When [Persona/Role] at [Company] [specific action], they discovered [shocking/surprising finding with specific numbers]. The [specific resource type] that [engineering/ops team] had [provisioned/configured/deployed] were [specific problem with quantification]. This was costing the company **[$X,XXX/month]** in [type of waste/unnecessary spend].

The problem [wasn't visible in standard tools / required X steps to diagnose / was politically sensitive]. [Specific challenge or barrier to solving it]. This incident became the catalyst for [specific solution or change].

<!-- EXAMPLE:
When Sarah, the FinOps lead at Acme Corp, ran her first right-sizing audit, she was shocked: **143 EC2 instances were overprovisioned**, running at an average of 18% CPU utilization. The m5.2xlarge instances that engineering had provisioned "to be safe" were costing the company **$22,300/month** in unnecessary spend.

The problem wasn't visible in CloudWatch alone — it required analyzing CPU, memory, network, and disk I/O together. And even when she identified the overprovisioned instances, getting engineering buy-in to downsize was a political minefield. This incident became the catalyst for implementing ZopNight's automated right-sizing recommendations.
-->

<!-- SECTION 2: BACKGROUND CONTEXT
     Explain why this problem is common and provide industry context.
     Requirements:
     - 2-3 paragraphs
     - Cite industry data or benchmarks if available
     - Explain root causes
     - Set up the solution that follows
-->

## Why [Problem] Happens

[Explanation of why this problem is common across organizations...]

According to [source, e.g., FinOps Foundation State of FinOps 2024], [relevant statistic with specific percentage or dollar amount]. [Additional context on industry norms, common anti-patterns, or contributing factors].

[Root causes: technical, organizational, process-related...]

<!-- SECTION 3: ARCHITECTURE DIAGRAM
     Show the "before" state visually.
     Requirements:
     - D2 or Mermaid diagram
     - Include cost annotations ($/month)
     - Use Zopdev brand colors (#0052FF, #00C48C, #FFB020, #FF6B6B)
     - Add fig-cap with descriptive caption
-->

## Current State

The diagram below shows the infrastructure before optimization. Notice the [specific elements to call out, e.g., overprovisioned instances, idle resources, etc.].

<!-- D2 DIAGRAM SYNTAX:
     Use {.d2} (with dot) NOT {d2}. The D2 Quarto extension is a Lua filter, not a built-in engine.
     - #| cell options are NOT supported — use inline attributes instead
     - For external files: {.d2 file="path/to/file.d2" width="100%"}
     - For inline code: {.d2 width="80%"}
     - ESCAPE dollar signs: use \$ not $ (D2 treats $ as variable substitution)
     - Path is relative to the QMD file, NOT the project root
-->

```{.d2 width="100%"}
direction: right

before: Current State {
  style.fill: "#ffebee"
  style.stroke: "#FF6B6B"
  style.stroke-width: 2

  cost: 45K dollars per month {
    shape: text
    style.font-size: 16
    style.bold: true
    style.font-color: "#FF6B6B"
  }

  resources: Overprovisioned Resources {
    shape: rectangle
    style.fill: "#ffffff"

    details: |md
      - 12 m5.2xlarge instances
      - 18% avg CPU utilization
      - No scheduling
      - 100% on-demand pricing
    |
  }
}

problem_indicator: High Cost / Low Utilization {
  shape: circle
  style.fill: "#FFB020"
  style.font-color: "#000000"
}

before -> problem_indicator
```

*[Architecture/workflow] before optimization*

The key issues visible in this architecture:

- [Bullet list of specific problems shown in diagram]
- [Quantify each issue: $X/month, N resources, X%]
- [Total impact: $X,XXX/month wasted]

<!-- SECTION 4: SOLUTION DEEP DIVE
     Explain the solution with production-ready code.
     Requirements:
     - Real, runnable code (not pseudocode)
     - Include imports and error handling
     - Add comments for non-obvious logic
     - Show sample output
     - Use language tag in code fence
-->

## The [Solution Name] Framework

Here's how we [solved the problem / implemented the solution]:

### Step 1: [First Step — e.g., Identify Overprovisioned Resources]

[Explanation of this step...]

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
[Brief description of what this script does]

Requirements:
    pip install boto3 pandas numpy

Usage:
    python script.py --region us-east-1
"""

import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from typing import List, Dict

def analyze_resource_utilization(resource_id: str, region: str = 'us-east-1') -> Dict:
    """
    Analyze resource utilization over the last 30 days.

    Args:
        resource_id: The resource identifier (e.g., instance ID)
        region: AWS region

    Returns:
        Dictionary with utilization metrics and recommendations
    """
    # PLACEHOLDER: Replace with your actual implementation
    # This is a skeleton showing the structure

    cw = boto3.client('cloudwatch', region_name=region)

    # Get 30-day metrics
    end_time = datetime.now()
    start_time = end_time - timedelta(days=30)

    metrics = {}
    for metric_name in ['CPUUtilization', 'MemoryUtilization', 'NetworkIn']:
        response = cw.get_metric_statistics(
            Namespace='AWS/EC2',
            MetricName=metric_name,
            Dimensions=[{'Name': 'InstanceId', 'Value': resource_id}],
            StartTime=start_time,
            EndTime=end_time,
            Period=3600,  # 1-hour intervals
            Statistics=['Average', 'Maximum']
        )

        if response['Datapoints']:
            metrics[metric_name] = {
                'avg': sum(d['Average'] for d in response['Datapoints']) / len(response['Datapoints']),
                'max': max(d['Maximum'] for d in response['Datapoints']),
                'min': min(d['Average'] for d in response['Datapoints'])
            }

    # Recommendation logic
    cpu_avg = metrics.get('CPUUtilization', {}).get('avg', 0)

    if cpu_avg < 20:
        recommendation = "Downsize 2 tiers or terminate if idle"
        potential_savings = 280  # $/month for m5.2xlarge → m5.large
    elif cpu_avg < 40:
        recommendation = "Downsize 1 tier"
        potential_savings = 140  # $/month for m5.2xlarge → m5.xlarge
    else:
        recommendation = "Current size is optimal"
        potential_savings = 0

    return {
        'resource_id': resource_id,
        'metrics': metrics,
        'recommendation': recommendation,
        'potential_savings_per_month': potential_savings
    }

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Example usage
    result = analyze_resource_utilization('i-0abc123def456')

    print(f"Resource: {result['resource_id']}")
    print(f"CPU Avg: {result['metrics']['CPUUtilization']['avg']:.1f}%")
    print(f"Recommendation: {result['recommendation']}")
    print(f"Potential Savings: ${result['potential_savings_per_month']}/month")
```

**Sample output:**

```
Resource: i-0abc123def456
CPU Avg: 18.3%
Recommendation: Downsize 2 tiers or terminate if idle
Potential Savings: $280/month
```

### Step 2: [Second Step — e.g., Implement Automated Policy]

[Explanation...]

```terraform
# PLACEHOLDER: Terraform or other IaC code
# See docs/CONTENT_QUALITY.md for examples

resource "aws_lambda_function" "right_sizing_enforcer" {
  function_name = "right-sizing-enforcer"
  role          = aws_iam_role.lambda_exec.arn
  handler       = "index.handler"
  runtime       = "python3.11"

  environment {
    variables = {
      THRESHOLD_CPU = "40"
      ALERT_SNS_ARN = aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn
    }
  }
}

# Add CloudWatch Event rule to trigger daily
resource "aws_cloudwatch_event_rule" "daily_audit" {
  name                = "daily-right-sizing-audit"
  schedule_expression = "cron(0 9 * * ? *)"  # 9 AM daily
}

resource "aws_cloudwatch_event_target" "lambda" {
  rule = aws_cloudwatch_event_rule.daily_audit.name
  arn  = aws_lambda_function.right_sizing_enforcer.arn
}
```

<!-- SECTION 5: INTERACTIVE CALCULATOR
     Add an OJS calculator to let readers model their scenario.
     Requirements:
     - Use Observable Inputs for user controls
     - Show reactive calculations
     - Style with Zopdev brand (.ojs-calculator, .ojs-metric)
     - Include static fallback for PDF/EPUB
-->

## ROI Calculator

Use this calculator to estimate potential savings for your environment.

::: {.callout-note}
## How to Use
Adjust the inputs below to match your resource counts and utilization patterns.
The calculator will estimate your potential monthly and annual savings.
:::

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// --- Input Controls ---

viewof instanceCount = Inputs.range([10, 1000], {
  value: 100,
  step: 10,
  label: "Number of instances"
})

viewof avgCpuUtilization = Inputs.range([0, 100], {
  value: 25,
  step: 5,
  label: "Average CPU utilization (%)"
})

viewof currentInstanceType = Inputs.select(
  ["m5.2xlarge", "m5.xlarge", "m5.large", "m5.medium"],
  {
    value: "m5.xlarge",
    label: "Current instance type"
  }
)

// --- Cost Model ---

instanceCosts = {
  "m5.2xlarge": 280,
  "m5.xlarge": 140,
  "m5.large": 70,
  "m5.medium": 35
}

recommendation = {
  if (avgCpuUtilization < 20) {
    return { type: "m5.large", action: "Downsize 2 tiers" };
  } else if (avgCpuUtilization < 40) {
    return { type: "m5.xlarge", action: "Downsize 1 tier" };
  } else if (avgCpuUtilization < 60) {
    return { type: "m5.xlarge", action: "Current size OK" };
  } else {
    return { type: currentInstanceType, action: "Current size OK" };
  }
}

monthlySavingsPerInstance = instanceCosts[currentInstanceType] - instanceCosts[recommendation.type]
totalMonthlySavings = monthlySavingsPerInstance * instanceCount
totalAnnualSavings = totalMonthlySavings * 12

// --- Output ---

fmt = d3.format("$,.0f")

html`<div class="ojs-calculator">
  <div class="ojs-summary-grid">
    <div class="ojs-metric">
      <span class="ojs-metric-value">${fmt(monthlySavingsPerInstance)}</span>
      <span class="ojs-metric-label">Savings per instance/month</span>
    </div>
    <div class="ojs-metric">
      <span class="ojs-metric-value">${fmt(totalMonthlySavings)}</span>
      <span class="ojs-metric-label">Total monthly savings</span>
    </div>
    <div class="ojs-metric">
      <span class="ojs-metric-value">${fmt(totalAnnualSavings)}</span>
      <span class="ojs-metric-label">Total annual savings</span>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="ojs-recommendation" style="margin-top: 1.5rem;">
    <span class="ojs-badge">Recommendation</span>
    <strong>${recommendation.action}:</strong> Migrate ${instanceCount} instances from
    ${currentInstanceType} to ${recommendation.type}
  </div>
</div>`
```

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
**ROI Calculator (Default Scenario)**

Based on 100 m5.xlarge instances at 25% average CPU utilization:

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Savings per instance/month | $70 |
| Total monthly savings | $7,000 |
| Total annual savings | $84,000 |

**Recommendation:** Downsize 1 tier — Migrate 100 instances from m5.xlarge to m5.large.

*Adjust values in the interactive HTML version for your scenario.*
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="epub"}
**ROI Calculator (Default Scenario)**

Based on 100 m5.xlarge instances at 25% average CPU utilization:

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Savings per instance/month | $70 |
| Total monthly savings | $7,000 |
| Total annual savings | $84,000 |

**Recommendation:** Downsize 1 tier — Migrate 100 instances from m5.xlarge to m5.large.

*Adjust values in the interactive HTML version for your scenario.*
:::

<!-- SECTION 6: AFTER STATE DIAGRAM
     Show the "after" state visually.
     Requirements:
     - D2 or Mermaid diagram
     - Include NEW cost annotations (showing savings)
     - Use green (#00C48C) for success/savings
     - Show before → after transformation
-->

## Optimized Architecture

After implementing [solution name], the architecture looks like this:

```{.d2 width="100%"}
direction: right

after: Optimized State {
  style.fill: "#e8f5e9"
  style.stroke: "#00C48C"
  style.stroke-width: 2

  cost: 27K dollars per month {
    shape: text
    style.font-size: 16
    style.bold: true
    style.font-color: "#00C48C"
  }

  resources: Right-Sized Resources {
    shape: rectangle
    style.fill: "#ffffff"

    details: |md
      - 8 m5.xlarge instances (right-sized)
      - 45% avg CPU utilization
      - Scheduled stop/start (nights + weekends)
      - 70% reserved, 30% spot
    |
  }
}

savings_badge: Savings 18K dollars per month (40%) {
  shape: circle
  style.fill: "#00C48C"
  style.font-color: "#ffffff"
  style.font-size: 14
  style.shadow: true
}

after -> savings_badge {
  style.stroke: "#00C48C"
  style.stroke-dash: 3
}
```

*[Architecture/workflow] after optimization*

Key improvements:

- [Bullet list of changes made]
- [Quantify each improvement: $X saved, N% reduction, etc.]
- [Total impact: $X,XXX/month savings]

<!-- SECTION 7: RESULTS & OUTCOMES
     Quantify the results with specific numbers.
     Requirements:
     - Dollar savings ($/month or $/year)
     - Percentage improvement
     - Timeline (X days/months to achieve)
     - Non-cost metrics (uptime, satisfaction, time saved)
-->

## Results After [Time Period]

After implementing [solution name] at [Company], the team achieved:

- **[$X,XXX/month in sustained savings]** ([X]% reduction from baseline)
- **[Zero/X]** [incidents/SLA breaches/performance issues] from [the changes]
- **[X minutes/hours]** average time to [key process improvement]
- **[X]%** [stakeholder] satisfaction with [the new process]
- **Break-even in [X] months** (initial implementation cost recovered)

**Key lessons learned:**

1. [First lesson with specific example]
2. [Second lesson with specific example]
3. [Third lesson with specific example]

<!-- SECTION 8: FIELD NOTES (Optional)
     Add practitioner insights or war stories.
-->

::: {.callout-tip}
## Field Notes

**[Specific insight or tip from implementation]**

[Brief story or example illustrating the insight. Include specific numbers or outcomes.]

For example: "We discovered that [specific finding]. By [specific action], we [specific improvement with numbers]."
:::

<!-- SECTION 9: CALL TO ACTION
     End with clear next steps for the reader.
-->

## Next Steps

To implement [solution name] in your organization:

1. **[First step]** — Use the [tool/script] above to [specific action]
2. **[Second step]** — Identify [specific resources/opportunities]
3. **[Third step]** — Start with [specific pilot or test]
4. **[Fourth step]** — Scale to [broader implementation]

**Timeline:** Most organizations achieve [specific outcome] within [X days/months].

**Resources:**

- [Tool/script name]: [Link or path]
- [Documentation]: [Link]
- [Support channel]: [Link or email]

---

<!-- SECTION 10: RELATED CHAPTERS (Optional)
     Link to related content in other chapters.
-->

::: {.callout-note}
## Related Chapters

- **Chapter [X]: [Title]** — [Brief description of how it relates]
- **Chapter [Y]: [Title]** — [Brief description of how it relates]
:::

<!-- END OF TEMPLATE
     Remember to:
     1. Replace ALL placeholders with actual content
     2. Test rendering: make render ebook=<slug>
     3. Validate quality: make audit ebook=<slug>
     4. Check diagrams render in HTML, PDF, EPUB
     5. Verify calculator works interactively (HTML) and has fallback (PDF/EPUB)
-->
